name: React deep dive (Artec)
description: The Artec frontend prep doc in depth: rendering model, re-render optimization, state strategy, SSR vs CSR, app structure, legacy migration, quality gates, API contracts, and AI-assisted development.

topics.name: React rendering model
topics.description: Render vs commit, reconciliation, keys, and what actually triggers a re-render.
topics.studyNotes:
React's rendering happens in two phases, and keeping them apart explains most of its behaviour.

The render phase calls your components to build a new virtual tree and diffs it against the previous one (reconciliation). It must be pure: React may run it many times, throw the result away, or interrupt it — so no side effects, no mutations, no reading refs mid-render. The commit phase then applies the minimal set of real DOM changes and runs effects afterwards.

What actually triggers a re-render:
- A state change in the component (a useState setter / useReducer dispatch).
- The parent re-rendering — children re-render by default, whether or not their props changed.
- A context value change, for every consumer of that context.

What does NOT trigger one: mutating a prop or an object in place. React compares by reference and never sees the mutation — a classic source of "why doesn't my UI update" bugs.

Reconciliation compares the trees node by node. For lists, keys tell React which items are the same across renders; index-as-key breaks when the list reorders, because state sticks to the position instead of the item.

Optimisation follows from the model: a re-render is cheap virtual-tree work unless the subtree is large. When it is, memoise it (React.memo, useMemo, useCallback) so reference equality lets React skip it — but measure first. Memoising everything adds overhead and hides the usual real problem: state living higher in the tree than it needs to.

topics.name: SSR vs CSR
topics.description: Server-side vs client-side rendering, hydration, and when a framework like Next.js earns its complexity.
topics.studyNotes:
Client-side rendering (CSR) ships an empty HTML shell plus a JavaScript bundle; the browser builds the page. Server-side rendering (SSR) generates the HTML on the server per request; static generation (SSG) does it once at build time. The choice is about who your first visitor is and what they need.

SSR/SSG earns its complexity when:
- Pages are public and content-heavy — crawlers get real, indexable HTML (SEO).
- First contentful paint matters — the user sees content before the bundle loads and executes.
- Content changes per request or per user but must still arrive as HTML (personalised landing pages).

CSR is the right default when the app lives behind a login: dashboards, admin tools, editors. There is nothing to index, the user already committed to waiting for the app, and SSR would add server infrastructure, deployment complexity, and cost for no visible benefit.

Hydration is the bridge and the catch: the server sends finished HTML, then React runs on the client, builds the same tree, and attaches event listeners to the existing markup. Until hydration completes, the page looks interactive but is not. Two costs follow: the work is done twice (server render + client hydrate), and any mismatch between server and client output (dates, random values, user-only data) causes hydration errors.

A framework like Next.js packages SSR, SSG, routing, and code-splitting so you do not build them yourself — but it brings a server runtime and its operational burden. The honest answer to "do we need Next.js?" starts with "is anything here public and SEO- or first-paint-critical?" — if not, plain React with CSR is the simpler system.

topics.name: Re-render Optimization & Memoization
topics.description: Measure first; React.memo/useMemo/useCallback, stable references, and rendering huge lists.
topics.studyNotes:
The senior framing comes first: MEASURE before optimizing — do not memoize by reflex. The usual real culprits are state living too high in the tree, props whose references change every render, oversized lists, and over-broad context updates.

The base strategy, in order: keep state as local as possible, avoid passing unnecessary props, split large components — and only then memoize.

The three memoization tools:
- React.memo — component level: if props did not change, skip re-running the child.
- useMemo — caches a computed VALUE between renders: expensive calculations, or a stable object/array prop.
- useCallback — caches a FUNCTION reference: needed for memoized children and effect dependencies.

The trap that ties them together: memoization compares by reference. If a parent creates a fresh object, array, or inline function on every render, the React.memo child re-renders anyway — the memo is defeated. So optimisation usually starts with cleaning the props API: pass less, keep references stable. Overusing memoization complicates the code and may not speed anything up.

## The one-million-items question

Never render them all. The first question is product-level: does the user actually need a million rows at once? Technically: backend pagination or cursor-based loading, and on the client virtualization/windowing (react-window, react-virtualized) so only the visible rows are mounted. Add stable keys and memoized row components where measurably useful. Pagination / infinite scroll / virtualization is the expected trio.

Related JS-level patterns worth naming: debounce (act after the input goes quiet — search fields) and throttle (act at most every N ms — scroll handlers) keep event-driven work off the hot path.

topics.name: Choosing a State Strategy
topics.description: Local vs shared vs server vs navigation state; prop drilling, Context+useReducer, and when Redux earns its place.
topics.studyNotes:
The senior answer starts with: there is no single state solution — different state TYPES want different tools.

- Local UI state (modal open, selected tab) → useState / useReducer.
- Simple shared state (theme, language, current user) → Context API.
- Complex app state (cross-module events, predictable updates) → Redux.
- Server state (API data, cache, refetch) → React Query / SWR — not a client store.
- Navigation state (filters, pagination, selected page) → the URL / router.

Prop drilling: passing state and setters down 1–2 levels is perfectly fine. It becomes a problem when the same state threads through many unrelated intermediaries — then reach for Context, Context+useReducer, or Redux by complexity. Senior touch: expose domain actions (login(), changeLanguage(), updateFilter()) rather than raw setters — updates stay controlled.

Context + useReducer is the lightweight Redux: Context provides access to a subtree without prop-threading; useReducer provides predictable updates. Split contexts by domain (AuthContext, LanguageContext) — one fat context value re-renders every consumer. What Context does NOT give you: DevTools time-travel, middleware, a selector layer, async orchestration.

Redux earns its place with complex shared state, multiple independent modules reacting to the same application-level events, and a need for predictable, debuggable updates. Reducers are pure state transitions; selectors hide the store shape from components and compute derived data (memoizable) — with a good selector layer the UI does not depend on the store's internal structure. Redux-Saga (or thunks/RTK listeners) is side-effect orchestration: sequencing, cancellation, retries, cross-module coordination — not just "API call library".

topics.name: App Structure & Reusable Components
topics.description: Feature-based boundaries, when a component deserves to be reusable, and working from Figma designs.
topics.studyNotes:
Route-based folders are fine for small apps, but in a large React app the route is just an entry point — real ownership follows FEATURE boundaries. The layout to describe: app/ (providers, store, routing setup), pages/ (route entry points that compose features), features/ (user, payments, dashboard — each holding its own components, hooks, API calls, selectors, state), shared/ (generic UI, utils, hooks, types). Components, logic and data access live close to their feature; only genuinely generic pieces graduate to shared.

Reusable component design — the senior stance is restraint: generalise when a UI/behaviour pattern actually repeats, not preemptively. A good reusable component has a clear API, predictable behaviour, sensible defaults, and no product-specific logic baked in. Warning sign: if it needs a dozen configuration props, it should probably be split into smaller pieces.

## Working from Figma

The happy path is the easy part; the interview points are in what is MISSING from the design:
- States: loading, error, empty, disabled, permission-denied.
- Responsive behaviour: mobile/tablet/desktop, long text, localization-stretched labels.
- Interactions: hover, focus, keyboard navigation.
- Reuse: does an existing component cover this, or is it a genuinely new pattern?
- Clarify edge cases with the designer EARLY — not when the sprint ends.

topics.name: Legacy Frontend Migration
topics.description: jQuery/Rails templates to React without a big bang, and modernizing an outdated React codebase.
topics.studyNotes:
The core principle: NO big-bang rewrite. Understand the existing behaviour first, find isolated areas where React can be introduced safely, and migrate in small reviewable steps — keeping current behaviour stable, adding tests around critical flows before touching them, and letting new functionality land in React while the legacy surface shrinks (the strangler pattern in practice).

What to refactor and what to leave alone: prioritise code that is actively changing, risky, hard to maintain, or blocking product work. Do NOT refactor stable legacy code for style points — a migration must reduce risk and serve product goals, not become a year-long rewrite with unclear value.

## Modernizing an old React codebase

- Dependency audit first: React itself, router, state library, build tooling, test libraries.
- Hunt deprecated APIs: legacy lifecycle methods, legacy context, ReactDOM.render → createRoot (React 18+).
- Class components do NOT get rewritten to hooks automatically — only the ones actively changing or causing problems.
- Test critical flows before AND after each step.
- Small steps in order: dependency update → build fix → tests green → pattern cleanup.

topics.name: Code Quality, Testing & Release Safety
topics.description: The quality tool layers, regression and visual regression testing, and feature flags.
topics.studyNotes:
Answer in LAYERS, from cheapest to most expensive feedback:

- TypeScript type checking — type errors before build/runtime.
- ESLint — static analysis: bad patterns, hooks rules, unused vars.
- Prettier — uniform formatting, fewer style debates in review.
- Unit/component tests — Jest/Vitest + React Testing Library.
- E2E tests — Playwright/Cypress on the critical user flows only.
- Visual regression — screenshot comparison (Loki/Chromatic) catching unintended UI changes.
- CI gates — typecheck + lint + tests + build + audit must pass before merge.
- Human layers — code review (design, maintainability, edge cases) and dependency audit (npm audit, Snyk, Dependabot).

Regression testing = verifying that a new change did not break previously working functionality; visual regression is the same idea aimed at how the UI looks.

## Release safety: feature flags

Deploying code is decoupled from enabling it: ship dark, then turn the feature on for internal users → QA → a small audience → everyone (gradual rollout). A kill switch turns a misbehaving feature off without a rollback deploy; flags also carry product-specific configurations and A/B tests. The cost to manage: FLAG DEBT — stale flags accumulating in the code; cleanup must be part of the process.

topics.name: API Contracts & Backend Collaboration
topics.description: REST semantics, when GraphQL helps, and the contract questions to settle with the backend early.
topics.studyNotes:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is a resource-oriented architectural style: resources identified by URLs, operations expressed by HTTP methods — GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates (full vs partial), DELETE removes. Do not just expand the acronym in an interview — describe the style.

GraphQL's honest pitch: it helps when the frontend needs control over the SHAPE of the data — when REST endpoints return too much (over-fetching) or too little (under-fetching, N+1 requests). It reduced payload sizes in targeted flows and makes data needs explicit. Equally honest counterpoint: REST is simpler and perfectly fine for most resource-based operations — do not claim GraphQL is always better.

## The contract conversation

Clarify the API contract EARLY with the backend team, before the frontend builds on assumptions:
- Request/response shapes — and what validation errors look like (error codes, error body format).
- The UI states the data implies: loading, empty, error.
- Pagination model: offset vs cursor.
- Permissions and auth: what returns 401 vs 403, and what the UI should do.
- Backward compatibility during migrations.

Settling these up front is what makes UI behaviour predictable — most "frontend bugs" around data are really contract ambiguities discovered too late.

topics.name: AI-Assisted Development
topics.description: Using AI as an accelerator while keeping engineering responsibility.
topics.studyNotes:
The position that lands well: AI is a development ACCELERATOR, and the engineer stays responsible for quality and correctness.

Where it genuinely speeds things up: boilerplate, refactoring suggestions, test generation, documentation, and exploring implementation options quickly. What does not change: generated code is not automatically correct — architecture, edge cases, types, accessibility, performance, security, and maintainability still get engineering review.

If the interviewer says "we barely write code by hand anymore", the productive response is curiosity plus principle: interesting — how do you organize the workflow around generated code? The essential ingredients remain strong review, tests, type checking, and architectural ownership, because AI accelerates implementation but does not remove the need for engineering judgment.

The failure mode to name: treating generation as a substitute for understanding. Code you cannot explain, you cannot debug, secure, or evolve — whoever (or whatever) wrote it.

questions.title: What triggers a React re-render
questions.prompt: Walk me through React's rendering model: what triggers a re-render, and what happens between a state change and the DOM update?
questions.answerStructureHint: State/prop/context change → render phase (reconciliation, diffing) → commit phase (DOM mutations); children re-render with parents by default.

expectedPoints.label: Knows what triggers renders
expectedPoints.description: setState, parent re-render, and context value change — not prop mutation by itself.

expectedPoints.label: Render vs commit, reconciliation
expectedPoints.description: Render phase builds the new virtual tree and diffs it; commit applies minimal DOM changes; keys drive list diffing.

expectedPoints.label: Render must be pure
expectedPoints.description: Render can run many times without side effects; effects run after commit.

followUps.prompt: Why is using an array index as a key a problem in a reorderable list?

questions.title: State management without Redux
questions.prompt: When do you actually need a state management library in React? How far do you get without one?
questions.answerStructureHint: Local state first, lift when shared, context for stable app-wide values, server-cache libraries for API data; a store only for complex shared client state.

expectedPoints.label: Local state first, lift when needed
expectedPoints.description: Keep state in the component that uses it; lift to the nearest common ancestor only when shared.
acceptedSignals: keep it close to where it's used

expectedPoints.label: Server data is not client state
expectedPoints.description: API data belongs in a server-cache tool (React Query/SWR) with caching and invalidation, not in a global store.
acceptedSignals: don't put api data in redux

expectedPoints.label: Knows when a store earns its place
expectedPoints.description: Complex, widely shared, frequently changing client state (or context re-render limits) justifies Redux/Zustand.

followUps.prompt: What is the performance pitfall of putting a frequently-changing value into React context?

questions.title: SSR vs CSR — do you need Next.js?
questions.prompt: When would you choose server-side rendering over a plain client-side React app? What does hydration mean in this picture?
questions.answerStructureHint: SEO and first-paint needs vs infra complexity; hydration attaches interactivity to server HTML; CSR is fine behind login.

expectedPoints.label: SSR for SEO and first paint
expectedPoints.description: Public, content-heavy pages need indexable HTML and fast first contentful paint; SSR/SSG delivers that.

expectedPoints.label: Explains hydration
expectedPoints.description: Server HTML becomes interactive when React attaches listeners client-side; mismatch and cost are the catch.

expectedPoints.label: CSR is fine for app-like UIs
expectedPoints.description: Dashboards behind login don't need SSR; SSR adds server infra, complexity and cost.

followUps.prompt: How do React Server Components change this trade-off?

questions.title: Optimizing unnecessary re-renders
questions.prompt: A React page re-renders too much and feels slow. Walk me through how you approach optimizing it — and how you would render a list of one million items.

expectedPoints.label: Measure first, structural fixes before memo
expectedPoints.description: Profile; keep state local, split components, trim props — memoization is not the first move.

expectedPoints.label: React.memo / useMemo / useCallback
expectedPoints.description: Component skip, cached value, cached function — each defeats a specific re-render cause.

expectedPoints.label: Stable references or memo is defeated
expectedPoints.description: Fresh object/array/inline function props defeat React.memo.

expectedPoints.label: Virtualization for huge lists
expectedPoints.description: Pagination/infinite scroll/windowing — only visible rows mounted (react-window).

questions.title: Which state goes where?
questions.prompt: You join a React project where everything lives in one global store. How do you think about what state belongs where?

expectedPoints.label: State types → different tools
expectedPoints.description: Local UI / shared / complex app / server / navigation state each has a right home.

expectedPoints.label: Prop drilling threshold
expectedPoints.description: 1–2 levels of props is fine; many unrelated intermediaries → Context or store.

expectedPoints.label: Context+useReducer and its limits
expectedPoints.description: Lightweight Redux alternative; split by domain; no devtools/middleware/selectors.

expectedPoints.label: When Redux earns its place
expectedPoints.description: Cross-module events + predictable updates; selectors decouple store shape.

questions.title: Structuring a large React app
questions.prompt: How would you structure a large React application, and when do you decide a component should become reusable?

expectedPoints.label: Feature-based boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Routes are entry points; components/hooks/API/state live inside their feature; shared layer for generic UI.

expectedPoints.label: Reuse on repetition, not prediction
expectedPoints.description: Generalize when the pattern repeats; clear API, sensible defaults, no product logic; too many props → split.

expectedPoints.label: Designs: hunt the missing states
expectedPoints.description: Loading/error/empty/disabled/permissions, responsiveness, long localized text; clarify early.

questions.title: Migrating a jQuery UI to React
questions.prompt: The product has a large jQuery/server-template UI and the team wants React. How do you run that migration?

expectedPoints.label: Incremental, no big bang
expectedPoints.description: Isolated areas first, small reviewable steps, behaviour kept stable, strangler-style shrinking of legacy.

expectedPoints.label: Tests around critical flows first
expectedPoints.description: Safety net before touching risky paths; verify before and after.

expectedPoints.label: Refactor by risk/value, not style
expectedPoints.description: Actively changing or blocking code first; leave stable legacy alone.

expectedPoints.label: Old-React modernization details
expectedPoints.description: Dependency audit, deprecated APIs, createRoot; class components only as needed.

questions.title: Frontend quality and safe releases
questions.prompt: What does your quality toolchain look like on a frontend project, and how do you reduce the risk of a release going wrong?

expectedPoints.label: Layered quality tools
expectedPoints.description: Typecheck → lint/format → unit/component → E2E on critical flows → CI gates before merge.

expectedPoints.label: Regression + visual regression
expectedPoints.description: New changes must not break existing behaviour; screenshot comparison for the UI.

expectedPoints.label: Feature flags for release safety
expectedPoints.description: Deploy ≠ enable; gradual rollout; kill switch; watch for flag debt.

questions.title: Agreeing an API contract with the backend
questions.prompt: A new feature needs new endpoints. What do you clarify with the backend team before you start building the UI?

expectedPoints.label: Shapes and error format
expectedPoints.description: Request/response shapes plus error codes and error body — validation errors included.

expectedPoints.label: UI states and pagination
expectedPoints.description: Loading/empty/error states; offset vs cursor pagination.

expectedPoints.label: Auth semantics and compatibility
expectedPoints.description: 401 vs 403 handling; backward compatibility during migration.

expectedPoints.label: REST vs GraphQL judgment
expectedPoints.description: REST for resource CRUD; GraphQL when the client must control data shape / avoid over-fetching.

questions.title: How do you use AI in development?
questions.prompt: How do you use AI tools in your development workflow, and where do you draw the line?

expectedPoints.label: Accelerator use cases
expectedPoints.description: Boilerplate, refactoring, test generation, docs, exploring options.

expectedPoints.label: Engineer keeps responsibility
expectedPoints.description: Generated code is reviewed for edge cases, types, security, performance, maintainability.

expectedPoints.label: Guardrails named
expectedPoints.description: Tests, type checking, code review, architectural ownership make generated code safe.
